AI.conclusion: The proposed tree search approach with hierarchical representation and PRM-guided MCTS effectively addresses the limitations of sequential decision-making, enabling more accurate and realistic text-to-3D indoor scene generation.

title: Large Language Models are Perplexed by some Political Parties
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used, including in political applications, but their political fairness has been little studied. We assess it using perplexity, posing that a fair model should give equal probability to all political groups. However, we find, across ten LLMs and three datasets covering 37 languages, that LLMs are more perplexed by the texts of far right and nationalist parties than of social-democratic parties. We find this to be consistent with previous work on translation fairness, to the point that perplexity correlates with downstream translation metrics. Our method is applicable to both base LLMs as well as their instruction-tuned counterpart, and we find that both are highly correlated, suggesting that the political fairness of LLMs stems from their pretraining, and is hardly affected by instruction-tuning.

AI.tldr: Study finds LLMs show bias, perplexity higher for far-right/nationalist texts vs. social-democratic ones, consistent across models and languages.
AI.motivation: Assess political fairness of LLMs, as their use in political contexts grows with little prior research on fairness.
AI.method: Used perplexity across 10 LLMs and 3 datasets in 37 languages to measure fairness, comparing probabilities assigned to different political texts.
AI.result: LLMs are more perplexed by far-right/nationalist texts, indicating bias, and this correlates with translation fairness metrics.
AI.conclusion: Political fairness in LLMs originates from pretraining, not instruction-tuning, showing consistent bias across models and languages.

title: The Self-Correction Illusion: LLMs Correct Others but Not Themselves
summary: Recent work shows that LLM agents struggle to correct errors in their own reasoning traces yet show markedly higher correction rates when identical claims appear under external sources. We ask whether this asymmetry reflects a capability deficit or a role-label artifact: does an agent's willingness to correct a wrong claim depend causally on the chat-template role that carries it, rather than on the claim's content? Our setup keeps the erroneous claim byte-identical across all conditions (SHA-256 verified) and varies only its wrapping role: the agent's own \role{<thought>}, a \role{user} message, a \role{tool} response, or a \role{system <memory>} block. Across 13 model-domain cells covering seven model families and three domains ($n{=}30$ paired tasks per cell), relabeling the claim from \role{<thought>} to an external role lifts the explicit-correction rate by 23 to 93 percentage points, with 10 of 13 cells reaching $p{<}0.001$. Further experiments confirm that the effect is asymmetric, mechanistically decomposable, and robust across domains. The failure to self-correct is not a cognitive deficit; it is a chat-template artifact. We exploit this artifact by designing a prompt-structure-only intervention that requires no training and no model modification, with its strongest role label being domain-dependent: \role{<memory>} dominates on math, while a plain \role{user} message dominates on logical deduction.

AI.tldr: LLM agents correct errors more when claims are labeled as external roles (e.g., user, tool) than their own thoughts, showing this is a chat-template artifact, not a cognitive deficit.
AI.motivation: The study investigates whether LLM agents' reluctance to correct their own reasoning traces is due to role-label artifacts rather than actual capability deficits.
AI.method: The setup keeps erroneous claims identical across conditions, varying only the wrapping role (e.g., agent's own thought, user, tool, system memory). It tests 13 model-domain cells and confirms robustness.
AI.result: Relabeling claims from the agent's role to external roles increased correction rates by 23-93 percentage points, with strong statistical significance in 10 of 13 cases. The effect is asymmetric and domain-dependent.
AI.conclusion: The inability to self-correct stems from chat-template artifacts, not cognitive deficits. A role-label-only intervention improves correction rates without model modification, with optimal roles varying by domain.

title: GenAutoML: An Agentic Framework for Dynamic Architecture Generation and Optimization in Time-Series Analysis
summary: Designing neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection remains a resource-intensive task that often requires substantial domain expertise. Traditional Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) systems typically rely on static, predefined search spaces, limiting their ability to adapt to diverse data characteristics. We present GenAutoML, an agentic framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as neural architects to bridge natural-language requirements and executable PyTorch implementations. The framework incorporates a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement and a Signature-Aware Runtime that enforces architectural consistency and execution safety. To improve robustness under non-stationary conditions, we further introduce a Dynamic Reversible Instance Normalization (Dyn-RevIN) wrapper. Experiments on the ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather benchmarks demonstrate that GenAutoML can dynamically generate task-specific neural architectures tailored to dataset characteristics. Among the generated models, WaveInterferenceNet achieves inference latency below 0.01 ms per sample while maintaining competitive predictive performance. By emphasizing computational efficiency, architectural adaptability, and stable optimization behavior, GenAutoML enables the creation of ultra-lightweight neural networks suitable for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive Edge AI deployments.

AI.tldr: GenAutoML is a framework using LLMs to automate neural architecture design for time-series tasks, featuring dynamic adaptation and robust performance under non-stationary conditions.
AI.motivation: To reduce the resource and expertise burden in designing neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection, and to overcome the limitations of static AutoML systems.
AI.method: GenAutoML uses LLMs as neural architects, a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for code refinement, and a Signature-Aware Runtime for consistency and safety. It also introduces Dyn-RevIN for robustness.
AI.result: GenAutoML dynamically generates task-specific architectures, with WaveInterferenceNet achieving sub-0.01 ms latency per sample and competitive performance on benchmarks like ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather.
AI.conclusion: GenAutoML offers efficient, adaptable, and lightweight neural networks, ideal for Edge AI deployments with resource constraints and latency sensitivity.

title: ReSAGE-PAR: Representational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
comment: Under review at IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology (TCSVT)
summary: To address the limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), we explore image synthesis using diffusion models guided by attribute-based prompts. While this enables the controlled generation of pedestrian images, it faces two critical challenges: (i) the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution, non-standard surveillance crops, and (ii) the need for reliable attribute verification to prevent generative hallucinations. In this paper, we introduce a robust generate-score-autolabel pipeline called ReSAGE-PAR (REpresentational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in PAR) that bridges this domain gap and enables scalable, high-fidelity dataset expansion. First, we adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. Second, we extract vision-language alignment scores between the generated images and their conditioning prompts, utilizing a comprehensive prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and inconsistent complements. Finally, we formulate a Bayesian classifier that converts these continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of ReSAGE-PAR in preserving spatial priors and verifying attributes. When integrated into PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR consistently yields significant improvements-achieving gains of up to 8.7% on standard backbones and pushing state-of-the-art frameworks to new performance levels. This proves its value as an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement. The complete codebase for ReSAGE-PAR is publicly available at http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/publications/ReSAGE-PAR.

AI.tldr: ReSAGE-PAR introduces a generate-score-autolabel pipeline to enhance Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) by bridging domain gaps and improving dataset diversity using diffusion models and Bayesian classifiers.
AI.motivation: The limited diversity and data scarcity in PAR necessitate robust methods to generate and verify high-fidelity pedestrian images while addressing domain gaps and attribute hallucinations.
AI.method: The paper uses a LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach for domain adaptation, vision-language alignment scores for attribute verification, and a Bayesian classifier for pseudo-labeling.
AI.result: ReSAGE-PAR achieves up to 8.7% improvement in PAR performance, preserving spatial priors and enhancing state-of-the-art frameworks.
AI.conclusion: ReSAGE-PAR is an effective, architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement, with publicly available code.

title: Measuring the sensitivity of LLM-based structured extraction to prompt, model, and schema choices in clinical discharge summaries
comment: 69 pages, 5 main figures, supplementary material included
summary: Large language models are increasingly used for structured extraction from clinical free-text notes, but the sensitivity of their output to upstream configuration choices is less understood than their accuracy on fixed benchmarks. This work measures that sensitivity without human-annotated ground truth, by holding the extraction task fixed and varying one choice at a time. The fixed schema comprises 17 clinical documentation flags on a three-way yes/no/not_documented value set and a 47-tag vocabulary for the primary admission reason. Three prompt variants expressing this schema were each run at two model sizes on MIMIC-IV v3.1 discharge summaries. Cross-prompt agreement was measured by Cohen's kappa on ICD-stratified subsets. A paired same-note comparison isolated the effect of model choice, and a post-hoc collapse of the three-way flags to binary tested the schema's contribution to disagreement. On the three-way flags, the two models reach the same pooled cross-prompt agreement (median kappa 0.69 and 0.68); the larger model raises agreement on some fields and lowers it on others, a redistribution rather than the absence of an effect. Collapsing the schema to binary dissolves most of the cross-prompt disagreement, locating it on the absence-versus-silence distinction rather than on whether the finding is present. On the multi-class admission categorization, changing the model reassigns the dominant tag on close to half of all notes while changing the prompt phrasing reassigns it on roughly one in eight, and the larger model places far less mass on residual catch-all categories (44% to 26%). These patterns indicate a schema-imposed source of disagreement concentrated on the absence-versus-silence axis and a dominance of model over prompt phrasing on multi-class categorization, identified by a reusable methodology for auditing extraction reproducibility on a population-scale deployment.

AI.tldr: The study examines how large language models' outputs vary with different configurations (prompt phrasing and model size) in clinical text extraction, finding schema design significantly impacts disagreement.
AI.motivation: To understand the sensitivity of large language models' outputs to upstream configuration choices in clinical free-text note extraction, without relying on human-annotated ground truth.
AI.method: Holding the extraction task fixed, the study varied one choice at a time (prompt variants and model sizes) on MIMIC-IV discharge summaries, measuring cross-prompt agreement and isolating the effect of model choice.
AI.result: Model size distributes agreement rather than uniformly improving it. Schema collapse to binary resolves most disagreement, pinpointing it to the absence-versus-silence distinction. Larger models reduce reliance on catch-all categories in multi-class tasks.
AI.conclusion: Disagreement is schema-imposed (on absence/silence) and model-dominated (in multi-class tasks), with a reusable methodology for auditing extraction reproducibility in population-scale deployments.

title: Framing, Judging, Steering: An Assessable Competency Model for Teach-ing Students to Reason With Generative AI
summary: Generative AI makes answers easy and understanding hard, and uncritical use invites cognitive offloading. Schools still measure unaided performance, yet the real task is to produce good work with AI: framing an ill-defined task, judging the output, and steering the model toward a better result. This ability is rarely assessed in its own right; where measured, it collapses into one "prompting" score that cannot diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. We propose CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning), a competency model factoring productive AI use into three assessable skills we abbreviate FJS: Framing (specifying an ill-defined task before invoking AI), Judging (evaluating output for errors and unstated assumptions), and Steering (iteratively redirecting the model). Its distinguishing claim is the separation of pre-generation Framing from post-generation Steering, with Judging as the gate between. We ground the skills in theory, state five testable propositions, and instantiate them in CoReasoningLab, an open platform that presents flawed AI output and scores them independently. Over simulated learners (generated and graded by different models), the skills dissociate: each tracks its own manipulated competence while staying flat in the others, and grades become correlated when one competence is shared across all three (convergent and discriminant validity), across grader backends from two providers. Human-rater agreement and outcomes are next; we release the instrument, data, and protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoRe-3, a model to assess AI reasoning skills (Framing, Judging, Steering) independently, demonstrating validity in AI-generated simulations.
AI.motivation: Schools focus on unaided performance, but the real challenge is using AI effectively. Current assessments often oversimplify AI collaboration into a single 'prompting' score, which fails to diagnose issues.
AI.method: Introduces CoRe-3, a competency model breaking AI use into Framing, Judging, and Steering. Validates it using CoReasoningLab, an open platform testing these skills with flawed AI outputs.
AI.result: Simulated learners showed the three skills dissociate, validating the model's structure. Grading correlated as expected (convergent/discriminant validity), and results held across different grader backends.
AI.conclusion: CoRe-3 provides a framework for assessing AI collaboration skills, addressing gaps in current educational evaluations. Future work includes human-rater validation and wider deployment.

title: Deciphering Two Training Clocks in Grokking via Deep Linear Network Theory with Conditional ReLU Reduction
summary: Grokking suggests that fitting the training data and learning a simple underlying rule may occur on different time scales. We formalize this phenomenon by separating the fast decay of the classification loss from the slower simplification of the learned representation, and we call the resulting pair of stopping times two training clocks. For deep linear networks, we show that a post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction condition reduces the cross-entropy loss to level epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. In contrast, when layerwise weight decay is present, the induced regularization on the end-to-end map can be expressed as a Schatten-type penalty; under a sharp late-time Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail, this structural energy closes on a polynomial time scale. The two clocks, therefore, separate fitting from representation simplification. We then explain how the same mechanism can appear in ReLU MLPs. In regions where the activation patterns on the training set remain fixed, the network reduces to a linear model in the active coordinates. In a two-layer ReLU embedding model, chain-rule estimates further show that the classifier head can receive larger effective gradients than the embedding block under controlled downstream norms. This supports a two-stage mechanism in which the classifier fits first, while the representation continues to simplify later. We use modular addition as the main experimental setting. The deep linear theory provides the rigorous core of the analysis. But the ReLU results are formulated as conditional reductions that account for empirical behavior without claiming a global proof for nonlinear training dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes the 'grokking' phenomenon, separating fast training loss decay from slower representation simplification, termed 'two training clocks'. It analyzes deep linear networks and ReLU MLPs, showing how fitting and simplification occur at different scales.
AI.motivation: To understand and formalize the 'grokking' phenomenon, where fitting training data and learning underlying rules happen on different time scales.
AI.method: Separates training into 'two clocks'—fast decay of classification loss and slower simplification of representations. Analyzes deep linear networks with post-margin gap-growth and weight decay, and ReLU MLPs where activation patterns localize the model to linear behavior.
AI.result: For deep linear networks, cross-entropy loss decays logarithmically under certain conditions, while weight decay induces Schatten-type regularization. ReLU MLPs show a two-stage mechanism: classifier fitting precedes representation simplification.
AI.conclusion: The 'two clocks' framework explains the separation of fitting and simplification in neural networks, supported by theoretical and empirical analyses in linear and ReLU settings.

title: Texture-preserving implicit neural representation for Cone beam CT truncated reconstruction
summary: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) frequently suffers from data truncation, which introduces severe artifacts and limits the effective field of view (FOV). Existing deep learning methods for truncated cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) reconstruction suffer from serious limitations, including a strict reliance on supervised ground truth and a failure to account for continuous 3D spatial truncation variations. To address these challenges, we introduce a self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations. By directly mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, our approach inherently bypasses traditional filtering and backprojection operations, thereby fundamentally eliminating truncation-induced ring artifacts while enabling robust continuous 3D data extrapolation. However, coordinate networks are susceptible to an inherent spectral bias, which leads to a severe loss of clinically vital high-frequency textures. To resolve this bottleneck, we further incorporate a physics-based iterative refinement module into the neural scene representation architecture. Leveraging the artifact-free, extrapolated volume from the coordinate network as an optimal initialization, this module progressively re-extracts and injects high-frequency structural information from the original projections back into the volume. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method successfully unifies the exceptional artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with the high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework using neural scene representations addresses CBCT truncation issues, combining neural network strengths with iterative refinement for high-fidelity detail preservation.
AI.motivation: CBCT data truncation causes artifacts and limits FOV. Existing deep learning methods lack flexibility in handling 3D truncation variations and rely heavily on supervised data.
AI.method: Proposes a neural scene representation framework mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity, eliminating truncation artifacts. Adds a physics-based iterative refinement module to recover high-frequency textures.
AI.result: The method effectively suppresses artifacts and enables robust 3D extrapolation while preserving high-fidelity details, validated on simulated and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies neural networks' artifact suppression with iterative algorithms' detail preservation, overcoming limitations of existing CBCT reconstruction methods.

title: Beyond Alignment: Value Diversity as a Collective Property in Multicultural Agent Systems
summary: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed in globally diverse settings, where different agents are grounded in different cultural backgrounds. Existing cultural evaluation focuses on value alignment: how closely a single agent matches a target culture. Yet alignment is a per-agent property and cannot reveal whether a system, taken as a whole, preserves the cultural plurality it is meant to represent. We propose value diversity as a system-level evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems, defined through the dissimilarity between culturally conditioned agents' responses on a shared value survey. Using the World Values Survey, we evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models across a wide range of system configurations. We find that diversity is largely uncorrelated with alignment, indicating that the two capture complementary system properties, and that current multicultural agent systems fall substantially below human societies in value diversity. Mixed-backbone systems narrow this gap but do not close it, and the gap persists across culture compositions and agent scales. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving agents toward consensus, and a participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization narrows the breadth of collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as a distinct evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems and reveal a persistent homogenization tendency in current LLM-based societies. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/iNLP-Lab/MultiAgent-Diversity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces value diversity as a system-level evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems, showing it's distinct from alignment and highlighting homogenization tendencies.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of system-level evaluation for preserving cultural plurality in multicultural multi-agent systems, focusing on value diversity.
AI.method: Proposes value diversity through dissimilarity in culturally conditioned agents' responses on a shared value survey, using the World Values Survey to evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models.
AI.result: Value diversity is uncorrelated with alignment, indicating complementary properties; current systems fall below human societies in diversity. Social interaction and consensus erode diversity.
AI.conclusion: Value diversity is a distinct evaluation axis for multicultural systems, revealing homogenization tendencies in LLM-based societies.

title: Beyond Vector Similarity: A Structural Analysis of Graph-Augmented Retrieval for Industrial Knowledge Graphs
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) fails systematically on queries requiring structural reasoning over interconnected entities. We compare eight retrieval architectures for aerospace supply chain intelligence, progressing from text retrieval through graph traversal to graph computation. Using a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges, we evaluate 23 queries across 10 intent categories and demonstrate that five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. Our central finding is the operator vocabulary thesis: the barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is not model intelligence but the computational operators available as tools. An LLM Query Planner with 9 typed traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) while generalizing to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph computation tools, the LLM selectively adopts them for exactly the query categories where traversal fails. We also identify a measurement gap: entity-level F1 systematically underscores structural queries where comprehensive answers are correct.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the limitations of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in handling structural reasoning tasks, particularly in aerospace supply chain intelligence. It evaluates eight retrieval architectures and introduces an LLM Query Planner with traversal and computation tools, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the systematic failure of RAG in queries requiring structural reasoning over interconnected entities, focusing on improving retrieval architectures for complex domains like aerospace supply chains.
AI.method: The research evaluates eight retrieval architectures using a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges. It tests 23 queries across 10 intent categories and compares vector retrieval with graph traversal and computation methods. An LLM Query Planner with 9 traversal primitives and 6 computation tools is introduced.
AI.result: Five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. The LLM Query Planner achieves higher performance (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) and generalizes to unseen queries. Graph computation tools are selectively used for queries where traversal fails. Entity-level F1 is found to underrepresent structural query accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The key finding is that the barrier to effective graph reasoning with LLMs is the lack of computational operators, not model intelligence. The proposed LLM Query Planner with traversal and computation tools significantly improves performance and adaptability.

title: TS-ICL: A Flexible Time-Indexed Foundation Model for Time Series via In-Context Learning
summary: Foundation models mark a profound paradigm shift in time series modeling, with task-specific models being superseded by general-purpose zero-shot models. Yet, current approaches primarily focus on forecasting, while real-world time series are often irregularly and partially observed, requiring models that can jointly forecast, impute missing values, and handle degraded sampling conditions. To address these challenges, we introduce TS-ICL, a novel probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder--regressor Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation. TS-ICL formulates time series tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and naturally incorporates covariates by training on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior. Empirically, TS-ICL achieves a new state-of-the-art in imputation, while remaining competitive with leading forecasting foundation models across both univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks. It shows particularly strong performance in forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

AI.tldr: TS-ICL is an In-Context Learning Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current approaches focus on forecasting, but real-world time series often have missing or irregular data, requiring models that can handle imputation and degraded sampling.
AI.method: TS-ICL uses a probabilistic encoder-regressor framework, trains on synthetic dependency structures, and formulates tasks as timestamp-aligned regression.
AI.result: TS-ICL excels in imputation and performs competitively in forecasting, especially with partially observed data.
AI.conclusion: TS-ICL addresses gaps in time series modeling by unifying forecasting and imputation, demonstrating strong performance across benchmarks.

title: LoomVideo: Unifying Multimodal Inputs into Video Generation and Editing
summary: Developing unified video generation and editing models capable of interpreting interleaved multimodal inputs is a promising yet challenging frontier field. Existing unified frameworks predominantly rely on massive models (typically 13B parameters or more) and incorporate source video conditions for editing by concatenating sequence tokens. This concatenation inevitably doubles the sequence length, quadrupling the computational complexity of the self-attention mechanism and introducing prohibitive overhead. To address these bottlenecks, we present LoomVideo, a highly efficient 5B-parameter unified architecture for both video generation and editing. LoomVideo replaces the standard text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs Deepstack injection mechanism to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Crucially, we introduce a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for video editing. By scaling and directly adding the clean source video latent to the noised target latent, this elegant design eliminates the need for token concatenation, drastically reducing computational cost while maintaining robust capabilities for complex, non-rigid edits. Furthermore, a Negative Temporal RoPE strategy is seamlessly integrated to handle multiple reference images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across comprehensive benchmarks, exhibiting exceptional superiority in e-commerce and fashion generation scenarios. Benefiting from the zero-overhead conditioning mechanism, LoomVideo achieves at least a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed compared to models of similar capabilities, paving the way for highly practical and efficient video foundation models.

AI.tldr: LoomVideo introduces a 5B-parameter model for efficient video generation and editing, using innovative conditioning and alignment techniques to reduce computational costs while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Existing unified video generation and editing models are computationally expensive due to reliance on massive models and inefficient token concatenation.
AI.method: LoomVideo uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and Deepstack injection for alignment, alongside a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for editing.
AI.result: The compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 5.41x faster inference speed compared to similar models, excelling in e-commerce and fashion scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LoomVideo offers a practical and efficient solution for video foundation models, addressing computational bottlenecks without compromising capabilities.

title: The Generator-Eraser Paradox: Community Guidelines for Responsible LLM-Assisted Dialect Resource Creation
summary: Dialect resources occupy a unique position at the intersection of scientific description, cultural preservation, and computational infrastructure. Large language models offer powerful capabilities for accelerating dialect resource development through retrieval-grounded drafting, corpus navigation, metadata enrichment, and annotation workflow support. However, the same systems pose substantial risks: they can contribute to dialect erasure by privileging prestige varieties, homogenizing orthography, and enabling synthetic feedback loops that reduce linguistic diversity over time. These risks are particularly acute for language varieties characterized by diglossia, limited written standardization, or marginalized speaker communities. This paper makes three contributions. First, we integrate insights from variationist sociolinguistics and corpus linguistics to formalize the generator-eraser paradox as a theoretical framework for understanding the dual nature of LLM-assisted dialect work. Second, we derive 12 community guidelines that operationalize this framework into implementable design requirements for dialect resource creation and documentation. Third, we provide an in-depth case study of Arabic dialects, including a structured comparison of widely used resources, to demonstrate how these guidelines address language-specific challenges including diglossia, orthographic variability, and community governance. The contribution is conceptual and operational rather than experimental, with the goal of enabling dialect communities and resource builders across languages to adopt LLMs without sacrificing authenticity, variation, or sovereignty.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the dual role of large language models (LLMs) in dialect resource development, highlighting both their potential benefits and risks, and proposes guidelines for ethical use.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the paradox of LLMs in dialect work—they can accelerate resource development but also risk erasing linguistic diversity.
AI.method: The paper integrates sociolinguistic and corpus linguistic insights to formalize the generator-eraser paradox, derives 12 community guidelines, and applies them to Arabic dialects as a case study.
AI.result: The study provides a theoretical framework (the generator-eraser paradox) and practical guidelines for using LLMs in dialect work, demonstrated through an Arabic dialect case study.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a conceptual and operational approach to help dialect communities and resource builders use LLMs ethically without compromising linguistic authenticity or sovereignty.

title: PLAN-S: Bridging Planning with Latent Style Dynamics for Autonomous Driving World Models
summary: Latent world models (LWMs) have strengthened end-to-end autonomous driving by forecasting compact scene dynamics for downstream planning. However, existing LWM-based planners usually generate trajectories directly from entangled latent representations. This compact latent-to-planner pathway lacks explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and diverse style preferences, making driving-style dynamics difficult to supervise, inspect, or modulate before a final trajectory is selected. We propose PLAN-S (PLANning with latent Style dynamics), a planner-facing bridge that addresses this compactness-controllability dilemma by decoding a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map from the latent representation. The cost map is conditioned on ego state and driving style and is consumed up-stream of the planning decision through two host-side interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. We validate PLAN-S on two architecturally distinct hosts, ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM, while keeping the host backbones frozen to isolate the contribution of the proposed bridge. On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 at every horizon over the baseline, with 0.55 m average L2 and a 42% relative reduction in the 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant reaches 89.4 Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS), while the learned cost variant provides complementary gains on baseline-challenging scenes. Ablations show that the cost pathway contributes most directly to safer trajectory selection. Qualitative results further show that PLAN-S can produce diverse cost maps, with spatially consistent variations aligned to different driving styles.

AI.tldr: PLAN-S introduces a style-conditioned semantic cost map to improve autonomous driving planning by addressing the lack of explicit risk and drivability modeling in latent world models.
AI.motivation: Existing latent world model-based planners lack explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and driving-style preferences, making supervision and modulation difficult before final trajectory selection.
AI.method: PLAN-S decodes a four-channel semantic cost map from latent representations, conditioned on ego state and driving style, and integrates it into planners via attention-level or reward-level fusion.
AI.result: PLAN-S achieves a 0.55 m average L2 reduction on nuScenes and 42% lower collision rate, while NAVSIM tests show improved performance with rule-cost and learned-cost variants.
AI.conclusion: PLAN-S enhances trajectory selection safety and diversity, demonstrating effectiveness across different planning architectures.

title: When Denser Credit Is Not Enough: Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Training
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents require reinforcement learning methods that can assign credit to intermediate decisions under sparse and delayed rewards. Recent group-based methods such as GiGPO improve over GRPO by constructing step-level advantages at repeated anchor states. However, we show that such dense credit can be statistically unreliable: under limited rollouts, rare but lucky actions may receive overly large advantages, producing divergent anchor bias and late-stage training oscillation. We propose Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization (ECPO), a critic-free policy optimization algorithm that calibrates step-level credit before policy updates. ECPO combines Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates, with Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B show that ECPO consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points on ALFWorld/WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B while adding only 0.1% additional advantage-computation overhead.

AI.tldr: ECPO is a critic-free policy optimization method that addresses unreliable step-level credit in long-horizon LLM agents by calibrating advantages before policy updates, outperforming baselines like GiGPO with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To improve credit assignment in sparse, delayed reward scenarios for long-horizon LLM agents by addressing the unreliability of dense credit in existing methods like GiGPO.
AI.method: ECPO uses Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage (grouping rollouts by actions and shrinking low-count estimates) and Variance-Gated Credit Weighting (suppressing noisy anchor states).
AI.result: ECPO outperforms baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points on ALFWorld/WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B, adding only 0.1% overhead.
AI.conclusion: ECPO provides a statistically reliable and efficient solution for credit assignment in long-horizon LLM agents, mitigating anchor bias and training instability.

title: LLM-Conditioned Synthesis of Pathological Gaits via Structured Gait-Language Representations
comment: Accepted at CVPR MOMA Workshop 2026 and selected for spotlight presentation at the workshop
summary: Pathological gait datasets remain scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability. Our work presents a multimodal LLM-guided framework for pathology-aware 3D gait data synthesis from structured textual descriptions. The proposed method generates fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences for pathological gait classification tasks. The framework combines motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. A key contribution is the proposed pathological tokeniser, which is designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning. Experiments suggest that the proposed synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. The best result is obtained using a GRU classifier trained with real and synthetic samples, achieving 92.77\% accuracy under a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

AI.tldr: A framework for generating synthetic 3D gait data from text descriptions improves pathological gait classification when combined with real data.
AI.motivation: Addresses the scarcity of pathological gait datasets due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and variability issues by creating synthetic data.
AI.method: Uses a multimodal LLM-guided framework with motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. Includes a pathological tokeniser to preserve motion characteristics.
AI.result: Synthetic sequences enhance classification performance, achieving 92.77% accuracy with a GRU classifier using real and synthetic data under leave-one-subject-out validation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively generates synthetic gait data, improving classification tasks when paired with real data.

title: Contextualized Prompting For Stance Detection On Social Media
summary: Stance detection on social media is challenging due to short, noisy, and context-dependent language. While large language models (LLMs) show zero-shot generalization, they are typically prompted without contextual information, which limits their ability to interpret ambiguous posts. In this work, we systematically investigate the impact of incorporating real-world (e.g., user biographies), derived (e.g., political party), and LLM-generated (e.g., target descriptions) contextual features into zero-shot prompting for stance detection on Twitter. Our evaluation spans four benchmark datasets, including a new high-quality German Twitter stance dataset. Across multiple LLMs, we find that integrating contextual information improves performance, but only under specific conditions. LLM-generated target descriptions consistently enhance accuracy, while other user metadata has mixed or even detrimental effects. Notably, we show that the inclusion of other tweets by the same user, often beneficial in supervised learning, can impair performance due to input noise. Our qualitative analysis reveals that LLMs struggle to distinguish task-specific useful information from irrelevant context. Our findings highlight both the promise and challenges of prompting with context information in noisy real-world settings. We publish code and data at this \href{https://github.com/tilmanbeck/stance-context-twitter}{page}.

AI.tldr: Incorporating contextual features in zero-shot prompting improves stance detection on Twitter, but effectiveness depends on the type of context. LLM-generated descriptions help, while user metadata can be detrimental.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the limitations of LLMs in stance detection by exploring how different contextual features influence performance, given the noisy nature of social media data.
AI.method: The authors systematically test the impact of real-world, derived, and LLM-generated contextual features on zero-shot prompting for stance detection, evaluating across four datasets, including a new German Twitter dataset.
AI.result: Contextual information improves performance under specific conditions: LLM-generated target descriptions boost accuracy, while other user metadata can harm performance. User tweets as context introduce noise.
AI.conclusion: Contextual prompting holds promise for stance detection but requires careful selection of context types to avoid noise, highlighting challenges in real-world noisy settings.

title: RedditPersona: A Modular Framework for Community-Conditioned LLM Adaptation from Reddit
summary: Community-conditioned language model adaptation requires choices about data collection, community definition, and evaluation that are currently made independently in each study, making it hard to compare assumptions or reuse artifacts. We present RedditPersona, a modular framework that standardizes these choices: it collects Reddit posts and comments, profiles active users, partitions them under five grouping strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, and interaction-based), trains a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA, and evaluates them under a shared metric suite spanning fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, and community identifiability. Applied to 112 subreddits in the urban well-being domain (301,429 user profiles, 16M+ comments), we find that adapters' behavioral identifiability tracks each strategy's intrinsic agreement with the subreddit baseline, and that a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text holds across all five strategies. The code and configuration files are available at: https://github.com/Ahghaffari/redditpersona.

AI.tldr: RedditPersona is a modular framework for community-conditioned language model adaptation, standardizing data collection, user profiling, grouping strategies, and evaluation. It evaluates five grouping strategies on urban well-being subreddits, revealing trade-offs between identifiability and text similarity.
AI.motivation: Current methods for community-conditioned language model adaptation lack standardized choices in data collection, community definition, and evaluation, hindering comparison and reuse of artifacts.
AI.method: RedditPersona collects Reddit posts and comments, profiles users, partitions them using five grouping strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, interaction-based), and trains adapters via QLoRA. Evaluation uses a shared metric suite.
AI.result: Adapters' behavioral identifiability aligns with each strategy's agreement with the subreddit baseline, with a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity across all strategies.
AI.conclusion: RedditPersona provides a standardized framework for community-conditioned adaptation, revealing insights into grouping strategies and their trade-offs, with code and configurations publicly available.

title: High-Dimensional Theory of LoRA Fine-Tuning in a Solvable Attention Model
summary: We develop a high-dimensional statistical theory of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, capturing the interplay between pre-training and fine-tuning. We introduce a solvable framework in which a single-head attention layer is first pre-trained on a data-abundant task and subsequently adapted via a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, both stages admit a sharp asymptotic characterization in terms of a finite set of order parameters, yielding explicit predictions for test errors and representation alignment. Our analysis shows that the impact of pre-training on LoRA is summarized by an effective noise term, from which we derive prescriptions for the optimal pre-training procedure. We also demonstrate a regime with a mismatch between the value of the test error and representation quality, and propose an application of our theory to active fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, focusing on pre-training and fine-tuning dynamics. It provides a solvable framework for studying these processes in high-dimensional settings, offering insights into test errors, representation alignment, and optimal pre-training strategies.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand how pre-training influences fine-tuning in attention models, particularly through LoRA. It seeks to provide theoretical insights into the interplay between these stages and their impact on model performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce a solvable framework where a single-head attention layer undergoes pre-training on abundant data, followed by a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. They use high-dimensional asymptotic analysis to characterize both stages with order parameters.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that pre-training's effect on LoRA is captured by an effective noise term. It also identifies a regime where test error and representation quality mismatch, leading to prescriptions for optimal pre-training.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a theoretical foundation for optimizing pre-training and fine-tuning in LoRA-based attention models, with practical implications for active fine-tuning strategies.

title: ReCache: Learning Budget-Aware Caching Schedules for Diffusion Models via REINFORCE
summary: Modern diffusion models generate high-quality images and videos, but their iterative denoising process makes inference expensive. Feature caching accelerates sampling by reusing or predicting intermediate activations across neighboring denoising steps, exploiting the redundancy of computations along the reverse trajectory. In this work, we focus on the caching schedule: selecting which denoising steps should be fully recomputed. Existing schedules are either fixed (e.g. uniform) or chosen adaptively from per-step error heuristics; in both cases, the actual compute cost is a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds rather than a quantity the user can specify. We propose ReCache, which inverts this: given a target budget k, it learns the recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality, turning compute into a directly controllable input. ReCache trains via policy gradients, sidestepping backpropagation through full diffusion inference, and uses no labelled data. Generations from uncached inference serve as matching targets, paired with a reward for generation quality. ReCache is compatible with any caching mechanism, including feature reuse and feature forecasting; for each mechanism, a single trained policy adapts across computational budgets at inference time. ReCache consistently outperforms scheduling baselines: under a $\times5.04$ FLOPs reduction on FLUX, it reduces LPIPS by 31% (from 0.456 to 0.316) compared to DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at a $\sim \times2.6$ speedup, it drops LPIPS by 65% (from 0.480 to 0.169) and boosts the VBench score by 7% (5.6 points, from 70.4 to 76.0) over uniform HiCache. Code is available at https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.

AI.tldr: ReCache introduces a learned recomputation schedule for diffusion models to optimize generation quality under a specified compute budget, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the high computational cost of iterative denoising in diffusion models by making compute a controllable input rather than a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds.
AI.method: ReCache uses policy gradients to learn a recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality within a given budget, without requiring labeled data or full backpropagation.
AI.result: ReCache reduces LPIPS and improves VBench scores significantly under reduced computational budgets compared to baselines like DiCache and uniform HiCache.
AI.conclusion: ReCache effectively balances compute cost and generation quality, offering a flexible and efficient solution for accelerating diffusion models.

title: EGTR-Review: Efficient Evidence-Grounded Scientific Peer Review Generation via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation
summary: Scientific peer review generation has attracted increasing attention for reducing reviewing burdens and providing timely feedback. However, existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based methods often produce generic comments with insufficient evidence support and weak source traceability, while complex multi-agent systems incur high inference costs. To address these challenges, we propose EGTR-Review, an Evidence-Grounded and Traceable Review Generation framework via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation. EGTR-Review first constructs a multi-agent teacher that performs structure-aware paper decomposition, key-element extraction, external scholarly evidence retrieval, evidence-state labeling, verification reasoning, and review synthesis. It then distills both intermediate reasoning trajectories and final review comments into a lightweight student model through task-prefix-driven multi-task learning. An evidence-weighted objective further reduces the influence of weak, missing, or non-verifiable supervision. Experiments on public peer-review datasets show that EGTR-Review (Student) outperforms strong prompt-based, fine-tuned, and structured/agentic baselines across automatic metrics, LLM-as-Judge evaluation, and human evaluation, while maintaining strong factual grounding and source traceability with substantially lower token consumption and inference time. Our code, prompts, configurations, and sample data are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: EGTR-Review is a framework for generating evidence-grounded and traceable peer reviews via multi-agent teacher distillation, outperforming existing methods in quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To reduce reviewing burdens and provide timely feedback by addressing the limitations of generic comments, weak evidence support, and high inference costs in existing LLM-based methods.
AI.method: EGTR-Review uses a multi-agent teacher for paper decomposition, evidence retrieval, and review synthesis, then distills knowledge into a lightweight student model via multi-task learning.
AI.result: EGTR-Review outperforms baselines in automatic metrics, LLM-as-Judge, and human evaluations, with lower token consumption and inference time.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves strong factual grounding and traceability while being efficient, demonstrating practical value for peer review generation.

title: Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents
summary: Despite recent progress, LLM agents still struggle with reasoning over long interaction histories. While current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason paradigm, this rigid pipeline design prevents them from dynamically adapting memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference. To bridge this gap, we propose MRAgent, a framework that combines an associative memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism. We represent memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph, where associative tags serve as semantic bridges connecting fine-grained cues to memory contents. Operating on this structure, our active reconstruction mechanism integrates LLM reasoning directly into memory access, allowing the agent to iteratively explore and prune retrieval paths based on accumulated evidence. This ensures that memory retrieval is dynamically adapted to the reasoning context while avoiding combinatorial explosion caused by unconstrained expansion. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark and LongMemEval benchmark demonstrate significant improvements over strong baselines (up to 23%), while substantially reducing token and runtime cost, highlighting the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.

AI.tldr: MRAgent improves LLM agents' memory reasoning by dynamically adapting retrieval with an associative memory graph and active reconstruction, reducing costs and boosting performance.
AI.motivation: Current memory-augmented LLM agents use a static retrieve-then-reason approach, limiting their ability to adapt memory access during inference.
AI.method: MRAgent uses a Cue-Tag-Content associative memory graph and an active reconstruction mechanism to iteratively refine memory retrieval based on reasoning context.
AI.result: Experiments show up to 23% improvement over baselines on LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks, with lower token and runtime costs.
AI.conclusion: Active and associative memory reconstruction enhances long-horizon reasoning efficiency and adaptability in LLM agents.

title: Addressing Imbalance in Multi-Label Data via Label-Specific Distance-based Oversampling
summary: The complex imbalanced label distribution poses a crucial challenge to multi-label classification, as most classifiers are biased towards the majority class and high-frequent labels. Oversampling is an efficient and flexible solution that augments instances to provide a more balanced training dataset for multi-label classifiers. Most existing oversampling methods create synthetic instances in a heuristic way that essentially relies on neighborhood information retrieved using Euclidean distance within the entire feature space. However, they fail to consider the varying semantic relevance of features to different labels, leading to label inconsistency among proximate neighbors and further introducing label confusion and overfitting to synthetic instances. To overcome the above issue, we propose a novel sampling approach called Label-Specific Distance-based Multi-Label Oversampling (LSDMLO) that creates more useful and well-labeled synthetic instances to address the imbalance in multi-label datasets. LSDMLO derives the label-specific distance to identify label-consistent neighbors based on the weighted pertinent feature space, which facilitates selecting seed instances that express more label correlations in boundary areas and generating synthetic instances aligned with the label distribution of original data. The comprehensive experiments verify that the proposed LSDMLO outperforms the state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches under various base classifiers.

AI.tldr: LSDMLO is a novel oversampling method for multi-label classification that addresses label imbalance by using label-specific distances to create better synthetic instances.
AI.motivation: Existing oversampling methods for multi-label classification rely on heuristic Euclidean distances, ignoring feature relevance to labels, causing label inconsistency and overfitting.
AI.method: LSDMLO uses label-specific distances to identify label-consistent neighbors and generates synthetic instances aligned with the original data's label distribution.
AI.result: Experiments show LSDMLO outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label sampling methods across various base classifiers.
AI.conclusion: LSDMLO effectively mitigates label imbalance in multi-label datasets by improving synthetic instance quality and label consistency.

title: FontFusion: Enhancing Generative Text in Diffusion Models with Typographic Conditioning
summary: Typography generation in diffusion models faces a persistent trade-off: enabling precise font control typically degrades text legibility, while maintaining readability often sacrifices typographic fidelity. We present FontFusion, a plug-and-play conditioning framework for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures that resolves this dilemma through three core innovations: (1) a hierarchical token representation establishing explicit text-font relationships at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings creating spatial bindings between typography and image content, and (3) a multi-level token dropping strategy improving both computational efficiency and generalization to unseen fonts. Our systematic evaluation of font embedding spaces reveals that a dual encoder combining DeepFont and DINOv2 outperforms any single encoder for typography tasks. FontFusion demonstrates 76% relative improvement on challenging decorative fonts over single-encoder baselines and font consistency gains exceeding approximately 68-76% over unconditioned models, while integrating into existing DiT architectures without retraining.

AI.tldr: FontFusion resolves the trade-off between font control and text legibility in typography generation using a novel framework with hierarchical tokens, position-aware embeddings, and token dropping.
AI.motivation: Addressing the persistent trade-off between precise font control and text legibility in typography generation.
AI.method: Introduces FontFusion, a plug-and-play framework with hierarchical token representation, position-aware embeddings, and multi-level token dropping for Diffusion Transformer architectures.
AI.result: Achieves 76% improvement on decorative fonts over baselines and 68-76% better font consistency, without retraining existing models.
AI.conclusion: FontFusion effectively balances typographic fidelity and readability, outperforming existing methods.

title: NAVIRA: Decoupled Stochastic Remasking for Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models generate text by iteratively unmasking many tokens in parallel, but this speed comes with a correction problem: tokens generated in the same step are predicted from marginal distributions, and early local dependency errors can later contaminate the context. PRISM addresses this by learning token-level quality scores and remasking unreliable tokens, but its inference rule is coupled: the same forward pass both detects low-quality tokens and computes logits for their replacements, so the erroneous tokens still condition regeneration. We propose NAVIRA, an inference-time decoding policy that separates these two operations and samples remasking positions stochastically. A first forward pass scores tokens; selected tokens are masked; a second forward pass regenerates from the cleaned context. Temperature-controlled remasking reduces repeated correction of the same positions and balances fluency against diversity. In controlled experiments with a 170M masked diffusion language model, decoupling improves fluency, while scheduled stochastic remasking preserves entropy and achieves stronger LLM-judge scores under larger forward-pass budgets. These results show that remasking policy, not only the learned quality signal, is central to reliable masked-diffusion text generation.

AI.tldr: NAVIRA improves masked diffusion text generation by decoupling token scoring and regeneration, using stochastic remasking to enhance fluency and diversity.
AI.motivation: Masked diffusion models suffer from errors due to parallel token generation and contamination from early mistakes, which PRISM addresses but with coupled inference. NAVIRA aims to improve this by separating detection and regeneration.
AI.method: NAVIRA uses two forward passes: the first scores tokens for quality, and the second regenerates masked unreliable tokens from a cleaned context. Stochastic remasking with temperature control prevents repetitive corrections.
AI.result: Experiments with a 170M masked diffusion model show NAVIRA improves fluency and achieves better LLM-judge scores under larger forward-pass budgets compared to PRISM.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the importance of remasking policy in reliable masked-diffusion text generation, showing NAVIRA's decoupled approach and stochastic remasking enhance performance.

title: Beyond Similarity: Trustworthy Memory Search for Personal AI Agents
summary:
Personal AI agents increasingly rely on long-term memory to provide persistent personalization across sessions. However, existing memory pipelines are largely driven by semantic similarity: memory data close to the current query is retrieved and injected into the model context. This creates a critical trustworthiness gap, since a semantically related memory may still be contextually inappropriate, leading to threats such as cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, or memory-induced jailbreaks.
  In this paper, we study memory search as a trust boundary in personal AI agents. We evaluate representative agentic memory frameworks, including A-Mem, Mem0, and MemOS, together with OpenClaw, a real-world personal-agent environment with persistent state and tool-use capability. Our results show that long-term memory is not merely a utility layer, but a durable control channel that can reshape how agents interpret tasks and execute actions, leaving them highly susceptible to the aforementioned threats. To mitigate these vulnerabilities, we propose MemGate, a lightweight and deployable memory plug-in for trustworthy memory search, with only 9M parameters and a 35.1MB footprint. MemGate is inserted between the vector memory store and the backbone LLM, requiring no LLM modification, memory-database rewriting, or inference-time LLM judge. It applies a query-conditioned neural gate to candidate memory representations, turning raw similarity search into task-conditioned memory admission. Across multiple mainstream memory frameworks, real-world agent settings, and diverse LLM backbones, MemGate reduces memory-induced threats while preserving long-term memory utility.

AI.tldr: The paper highlights the risks of current memory pipelines in personal AI agents, proposes MemGate to mitigate threats, and demonstrates its effectiveness across various frameworks.
AI.motivation: To address the trustworthiness gap in long-term memory systems of personal AI agents, which can lead to issues like cross-domain leakage and memory-induced jailbreaks.
AI.method: Evaluate existing memory frameworks (A-Mem, Mem0, MemOS) and introduce MemGate, a lightweight plug-in that uses a query-conditioned neural gate to filter memory inputs.
AI.result: MemGate effectively reduces memory-induced threats without modifying the LLM or memory database, maintaining utility across diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: Long-term memory is a durable control channel requiring safeguards like MemGate to ensure trustworthy and secure AI agent performance.

title: Short paper: Models in the dark -- Rectification and erasure under GDPR in ML supply chains
comment: accepted for presentation at Annual Privacy Forum 2026
summary: The rights to rectification and erasure, as established under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), are central to protecting individuals' privacy. However, their effective enforcement in machine learning (ML) systems remains challenging. Existing work has largely addressed these rights from either a legal or a technical perspective in isolation and disregards the fact that models are produced in complex supply chains involving multiple actors across development, distribution, and deployment. This paper presents a holistic survey of challenges in implementing the rights to rectification and erasure in ML models. Drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, we find that many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice. Our findings further suggest that issues arising in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed in research. To tackle this gap, we introduce the notion of models in the dark -- derived models created further downstream in an ML chain without sufficient transparency or traceability -- and analyse the urgent challenges posed by this phenomenon. By adopting an interdisciplinary perspective, this work contributes to bridging the gap between legal requirements and the technical implementation of data subject rights in ML, ultimately supporting the development of trustworthy artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper examines challenges in enforcing GDPR's rectification and erasure rights in ML systems, highlighting gaps in addressing ML supply chain complexities and introducing the concept of 'models in the dark.'
AI.motivation: To address the disconnect between legal requirements and technical feasibility in implementing GDPR rights within ML systems, especially considering the multi-actor supply chains involved.
AI.method: A holistic survey combining academic literature and data protection authority guidance, focusing on the implementation challenges of rectification and erasure rights in ML.
AI.result: Many GDPR requirements are technically unfeasible in current ML systems, and ML supply chain issues are under-researched. The paper introduces 'models in the dark' as a critical challenge.
AI.conclusion: The work advocates for interdisciplinary approaches to bridge legal and technical gaps, aiming to foster trustworthy AI development.

title: VZCrash: A Large-Scale IMU Dataset of Ego-Vehicle Crashes
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC 2026). VZCrash is publicly available at this URL: https://huggingface.co/datasets/vzc-research-chapter/VZCrash
summary: We introduce VZCrash, the largest publicly available dataset of real-world vehicle collision data featuring Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) telemetry. The dataset contains more than 31,000 validated crashes and 158,000 negative samples, including hard cases and distractors. Each sample includes acceleration and angular velocity at 100 Hz, and GPS speed at 1 Hz. Events in VZCrash were captured by devices installed on a fleet of 73,010 commercial vehicles of different sizes driving in the United States over the span of several years. We also present an extensive experimental study enabled by the volume of the dataset. We first benchmark several different approaches, from a simple threshold-based heuristic to state-of-the-art deep learning models. Then, we present an experiment demonstrating the importance of scaling data to train high-quality crash detection models, and we show that scale is especially important when these models need to be deployed into a real-world environment.

AI.tldr: VZCrash is the largest public dataset of real-world vehicle collision data with IMU telemetry, featuring 31,000 crashes and 158,000 negative samples. It includes extensive experiments showing the importance of data scale for training effective crash detection models.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive and publicly available dataset of real-world vehicle collision data, enabling the development and benchmarking of crash detection models.
AI.method: Collected data from 73,010 commercial vehicles in the U.S. over several years, including IMU telemetry (100 Hz) and GPS speed (1 Hz). Conducted experiments benchmarking various approaches, from threshold-based heuristics to deep learning models.
AI.result: Demonstrated the importance of large-scale data for training high-quality crash detection models, especially for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: VZCrash dataset and experiments highlight the critical role of data scale in improving crash detection models, offering valuable insights for future research and applications.

title: English-to-Prakrit Machine Translation via Multilingual Transfer Learning
summary: We study English-to-Prakrit machine translation in a low-resource setting where the target language is unsupported by IndicTrans2. We adapt the multilingual model by mapping Prakrit to the Hindi language tag (hin_Deva) without modifying the tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture. Using a 1,474-pair Maharashtri Prakrit parallel corpus and evaluation on a 20-sample Ardhamagadhi test set, we report corpus BLEU improvements over an untuned baseline. The results indicate that script-compatible language routing can enable feasible transfer to unsupported classical languages, while highlighting limitations due to data scarcity and dialect mismatch. Our code and trained models are released to the public for further exploration https://github.com/D3v1s0m/indictrans2-prakrit-mt.

AI.tldr: The study adapts an existing multilingual model for English-to-Prakrit translation by mapping Prakrit to Hindi, showing improvements in BLEU scores despite low-resource constraints.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of machine translation for unsupported classical languages like Prakrit, leveraging existing multilingual models to fill the gap.
AI.method: The authors adapt the IndicTrans2 model by mapping Prakrit to the Hindi language tag (hin_Deva) without altering the model's tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture. They use a small parallel corpus for training and evaluate on a test set.
AI.result: The approach yields improved BLEU scores over an untuned baseline, demonstrating feasibility despite data scarcity and dialect mismatch.
AI.conclusion: Script-compatible language routing can enable translation for unsupported classical languages, though limitations due to low resources remain. The code and models are publicly released for further research.

title: When Should Memory Stay Silent: Measuring Memory-Use Boundaries in Memory-Augmented Conversational Agents
summary: Long-term memory enables language model agents to support personalized interactions, but it remains unclear when available memories warrant integration into responses. Existing memory evaluations emphasize retrieval accuracy and downstream task utility, while overlooking whether retrieved sensitive memory content is warranted in the current turn. We introduce RBI-Eval, a controlled measurement study built around a probe set that compares model behavior with and without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts. We evaluate four base LLMs against a matched no-memory reference across four memory-access settings: full-context exposure and three retrieval systems. Our results reveal substantial behavioral divergence. With memory available, the separation score for sensitive-memory integration decreases by 8.9\%--26.6\% relative to the matched no-memory reference for GPT-5.4-mini, but by 51.1\%--82.9\% for Claude-Sonnet-4.6, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Qwen3.5-9B. Control experiments on DeepSeek and GPT-5.4-mini show this effect is specific to sensitive content, rather than general personalization. Retrieval systems reduce exposure but do not eliminate integration once sensitive memory reaches the generator. These findings suggest safe personalization requires memory-aware decisions at both retrieval and generation time.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RBI-Eval to study how sensitive memory content in language models affects response integration, revealing significant behavioral divergence and the need for memory-aware decisions in retrieval and generation.
AI.motivation: To understand when and how sensitive memory content should be integrated into responses by language model agents, as existing evaluations overlook the appropriateness of memory integration.
AI.method: The study uses RBI-Eval, a probe set comparing model behavior with and without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts. It evaluates four LLMs across different memory-access settings.
AI.result: Results show significant behavioral divergence, with sensitive-memory integration decreasing by varying percentages across models. Retrieval systems reduce but do not eliminate integration of sensitive content.
AI.conclusion: Safe personalization requires memory-aware decisions at both retrieval and generation stages to manage sensitive content effectively.

title: Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning
summary: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges singular learning theory and information geometry by analyzing the 'dead direction'—a unit vector where the Fisher metric degenerates—and shows its KL order is recoverable without complex resolutions.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to unify singular learning theory and information geometry, which study parameter spaces separately, by focusing on the 'dead direction' to provide insights into Watanabe's invariants and practical applications like deep networks.
AI.method: The authors analyze the 'dead direction' and its KL order, linking it to the decay rate of directional Fisher curvature. They extend this to multi-component crossings, multiplicity, and singular fluctuation, and apply it to deep networks using K-FAC factorization.
AI.result: The work provides a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form predictions per architecture, and a way to compute Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from a single checkpoint's forward and backward passes.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully bridges the gap between singular learning theory and information geometry, offering practical tools and theoretical insights for analyzing singularities in deep learning.

title: Knowledge Distillation for Visual Autoregressive Models
summary: Autoregressive (AR) image generation models are highly expressive but computationally intensive, motivating effective model compression. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a natural approach for model compression and has been widely studied in language modeling, yet its behavior in visual AR generation remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of distillation strategies for AR image models. Our analysis shows that while standard distillation can yield meaningful gains, recent methods developed for language do not directly transfer to images: long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity make teacher supervision unreliable especially under student-conditioned contexts. To address this, we propose VarKD, a distillation framework for visual autoregressive models that distills on student samples while selectively applying teacher supervision and reducing token-level ambiguity. Experiments on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones show that VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.

AI.tldr: This paper studies distillation strategies for autoregressive (AR) image generation models, introducing VarKD to address challenges like long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity.
AI.motivation: AR image models are computationally intensive, but knowledge distillation (KD) for them is underexplored compared to language models. The study aims to adapt KD effectively for visual AR generation.
AI.method: The paper introduces VarKD, a distillation framework that selectively applies teacher supervision on student samples and reduces token-level ambiguity.
AI.result: VarKD outperforms prior distillation baselines on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.
AI.conclusion: VarKD provides a systematic and effective approach for distilling AR image models, addressing unique challenges in visual generation.

title: IA-RAG: Interval-Algebra-Driven Temporal Reasoning for Dynamic Knowledge Retrieval
